original_hypothesis.title: Gauge-enabled markets may be non-functional due to whitelist key mismatch (market vs gauge address) causing sync_ledger/withdraw to revert
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When a market is whitelisted with _hasGauge=true, LendingLedger deploys a LiquidityGauge and stores a bi-directional mapping. However, sync_ledger rewrites the effective market key to the LiquidityGauge address, while update_market enforces the whitelist on that rewritten key. Since whiteListLendingMarket only sets lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] (the underlying market), the LiquidityGauge address may not be whitelisted, making update_market revert for all gauge token transfers/mints/burns. This can brick deposits/withdrawals/transfers for gauge-enabled markets.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete, highly realistic integration bug that can halt all gauge-based flows (including withdrawUnderlying) without any special attacker capabilities. It also matches the Phase A logic-analysis 'assumption violation' around whitelist resolution and LiquidityGauge callback coupling.

proof_of_signal.lines: sync_ledger(): lendingMarket = liquidityGauges[msg.sender] when nonzero; then update_market(lendingMarket) requires lendingMarketWhitelist[lendingMarket]
proof_of_signal.pattern: whitelist set on _market, but runtime key becomes LiquidityGauge address

preconditions: Governance called whiteListLendingMarket(_market, true, true) so a LiquidityGauge exists
LiquidityGauge token transfer/mint/burn triggers _afterTokenTransfer which calls LendingLedger.sync_ledger
lendingMarketWhitelist is not set for the LiquidityGauge address (only for the underlying market)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LendingLedger.sol sync_ledger + update_market whitelist check; LendingLedger.sol whiteListLendingMarket only whitelists _market; LiquidityGauge.sol _afterTokenTransfer always calls sync_ledger
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: inconsistent market identifier between whitelisting and accounting key
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: any gauge token movement reverts due to whitelist failure, preventing withdrawUnderlying and transfers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no normalization ensuring update_market checks whitelist against the underlying market key

analysis_questions: For a gauge-enabled market, does sync_ledger pass the gauge address or underlying market to update_market?
Which address does governance actually add as a gauge in GaugeController (market or LiquidityGauge)?
Can governance realistically work around this by whitelisting the LiquidityGauge address as well, and does that introduce other inconsistencies?
validation_requirements: Write an integration test: whitelist market with _hasGauge=true, attempt depositUnderlying/withdrawUnderlying, observe revert source
Confirm mapping direction: liquidityGauges[market]=gauge and liquidityGauges[gauge]=market, and the chosen key in sync_ledger
Check whether lendingMarketTotalBalance and marketInfo keys align across all entry points (sync_ledger, claim, update_market)

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any user tries to depositUnderlying/withdrawUnderlying/transfer gauge tokens; _afterTokenTransfer calls sync_ledger; sync_ledger resolves lendingMarket to the LiquidityGauge address; update_market reverts on 'Market not whitelisted'; user actions become impossible, effectively locking underlying in the gauge for all participants.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users may be unable to withdraw underlying from LiquidityGauge (hard lock) and/or unable to interact with the market reward accounting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only via governance workaround/migration; no on-chain user escape hatch

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end tests for gauge-enabled market flows (whitelist -> depositUnderlying -> withdrawUnderlying).

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: reverts observable immediately on first gauge token transfer

validation_result.impact_summary: No mismatch exists. The bi-directional mapping correctly resolves the gauge address back to the underlying market address, so the whitelist check passes.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that sync_ledger rewrites the effective market key to the LiquidityGauge address, causing update_market's whitelist check to fail. This is factually incorrect based on careful code tracing. When LiquidityGauge calls sync_ledger (msg.sender = gaugeAddress), the mapping liquidityGauges[gaugeAddress] returns _market (the underlying market address), NOT the gauge address. The whitelist is set on _market, and the resolved key is also _market, so the check passes correctly.

The bi-directional mapping works as designed:
- liquidityGauges[_market] = gaugeAddress (forward)
- liquidityGauges[gaugeAddress] = _market (reverse)

When the gauge calls sync_ledger, the reverse mapping is used, correctly resolving back to the whitelisted market address.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The hypothesis misidentifies the direction of the mapping resolution. Line-by-line tracing shows the mapping resolves gauge→market (not gauge→gauge), which aligns with the whitelist key. All state mappings (marketInfo, userInfo, lendingMarketTotalBalance) are consistently keyed on _market after resolution.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: NOT VULNERABLE: Sets liquidityGauges[gaugeAddr] = _market and lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] = true. Both use _market as the canonical key.

NOT VULNERABLE: When msg.sender is the gauge address, liquidityGauges[gaugeAddr] returns _market (the original underlying market), so lendingMarket becomes _market, not the gauge address. update_market(_market) then correctly finds lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] == true.

calling_sequence: 4. _afterTokenTransfer calls LendingLedger.sync_ledger(user, int256(amount)) with msg.sender = gaugeAddress
7. update_market(_market): checks lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] which is TRUE
8. All subsequent state reads/writes use _market as key: marketInfo[_market], userInfo[_market][user], lendingMarketTotalBalance[_market]
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The hypothesized vulnerability does not exist. The mapping resolution in sync_ledger at line 102 correctly maps gaugeAddress → _market (reverse direction), not gaugeAddress → gaugeAddress as the hypothesis assumes. Since whiteListLendingMarket sets lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] = true and sync_ledger resolves to _market, the whitelist check in update_market passes. The code at LendingLedger.sol:144 explicitly sets the reverse mapping: liquidityGauges[address(liquidityGauge)] = _market, which is the mapping read at line 102.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for market whitelisting; the Deploy.sol only deploys the core contracts. However, the hypothesis is refuted purely based on code logic, not configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario described — where depositUnderlying/withdrawUnderlying reverts due to whitelist failure — cannot occur through the gauge callback path. When LiquidityGauge._afterTokenTransfer calls sync_ledger, msg.sender is the gauge address. The reverse mapping liquidityGauges[gaugeAddress] correctly returns the original _market address. update_market(_market) finds lendingMarketWhitelist[_market] == true and proceeds normally. The hypothesis fundamentally misidentifies the direction of the mapping resolution.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The code correctly handles the gauge-to-market address resolution. The bi-directional mapping is explicitly designed for this purpose (comment on line 143: '// add reverse also for reference in sync_ledger'). All gauge-enabled market flows (deposit, withdraw, transfer) should function as intended through the mapping resolution.
validation_result.replication_plan: Deploy LendingLedger, GaugeController, VotingEscrow. Whitelist a market with _hasGauge=true. Set rewards. Deposit underlying into the LiquidityGauge. Verify that sync_ledger resolves to the correct market address and does not revert. The test should pass, confirming the hypothesis is false.
uncertainties: If the underlying market contract itself directly calls sync_ledger (rather than users going through the gauge), then liquidityGauges[_market] = gaugeAddress, so lendingMarket becomes gaugeAddress, and update_market(gaugeAddress) would fail the whitelist check. This is a different scenario from the hypothesis, and the design intent is that once a gauge exists, all interactions go through the gauge.
additional_testing_needed: End-to-end integration test for the full gauge flow to confirm no other issues exist
Test what happens if the underlying market directly calls sync_ledger when a gauge exists (separate concern from hypothesis)
